title: Who is using AI to code? Global diffusion and impact of generative AI
study_design_description: An observational study analyzing over 30 million software developer contributions (commits) to open-source Python projects on GitHub. The study uses a machine learning classifier to identify code written with substantial AI assistance. Data were collected from approximately 100,000 US GitHub users and a random sample of 2,000 programmers per year from five other major countries (China, France, Germany, India, Russia). To determine the effects of AI adoption on developer output, the study relies on regression models with user and quarter-of-year fixed effects, comparing the output of the same programmer at different points of AI adoption while controlling for economy-wide trends.
primary_task_description: Analysis of code commits to open-source Python projects, focusing on blocks of code representing functions as fine-grained, self-contained units. The study assesses how generative AI impacts the quantity and nature of code that programmers produce, measured in terms of quarterly number of commits and library usage patterns.
population_description: Approximately 100,000 US GitHub users contributing to Python-based open-source projects, plus a random sample of 2,000 programmers per year from each of five other major countries in software development (China, France, Germany, India, Russia). The full dataset comprises 100,097 users tracked over an average time span of 5.1 quarters. Analysis shows that AI adoption rates vary by developer experience: experienced developers use genAI in 27% of their code, while programmers who have just joined GitHub use these tools for 37% of code. No significant gender differences were found.

outcomes.outcome_description: Percentage of Python functions generated by AI, assessed through machine learning classification of commits
Quarterly number of all commits by a user
Commits that make changes to multiple files (scripts) in a project, requiring navigation of project-level dependencies
Commits that add library imports to a script
Number of unique libraries that a user adds across all their commits in a given quarter
Number of unique libraries that a user adds in a quarter that had not been used in any prior quarter by the same user

reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: About 29% of Python functions in the US were generated by AI by the end of 2024, closely followed by 23/24% for Germany and France. India draws close at 20%, after having initially lagged in adoption.

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: 27% for experienced developers, 37% for inexperienced developers
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Whereas the most experienced developers use genAI in 27% of their code, programmers who have just joined the GitHub platform use these tools for 37% of code.
Moving from 0 to 29% genAI usage—the estimated US adoption rate by the end of 2024—is associated with a 3.6% increase in commit rates across all these commit types.

However, these associations with user productivity are fully driven by experienced users, for whom a 29% adoption rate would imply a 6.2% increase in commit rates (Fig. 3D). In contrast, we observe no statistically significant effects among inexperienced users.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: at average end-of-2024 AI use rates for US developers
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: At average end-of-2024 AI use rates for US developers, our models predict that developers will implement 2.7% more new combinations of libraries.
The classifier performs remarkably well, achieving an out-of-sample ROC AUC Score of 0.96 (Fig. S2D) and Average Rate of True positives of 0.95.
reaching an out-of-sample ROC AUC Score of 0.96, Average Precision Score [52] of 0.9685 and Average true positive rate of 0.95 (F1 score 0.8911 with a 0.5 threshold).

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: classifier true positive rates by LLM type
reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: GPT-3.5 turbo synthetic: 0.701, GPT-3.5 turbo assisted: 0.698, GPT-4 synthetic: 0.675, GPT-4 assisted: 0.651
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: The average probability of true positives is very similar across samples at around 0.7 (GPT-3.5 turbo — synthetic: 0.701, assisted: 0.698; GPT-4 — synthetic: 0.675, assisted: 0.651).

notes: This is an observational study using machine learning classification to detect AI-generated code in GitHub commits. The study tracks AI adoption patterns across countries and analyzes productivity effects using within-developer variation (fixed effects regression). The paper includes extensive supplementary materials with robustness checks, measurement error analysis, and general equilibrium calculations. Key finding: experienced programmers capture nearly all productivity and exploration gains from AI, widening rather than closing skill gaps. The study notes that p-values of AI use range from 0.11 to 0.93 across different model specifications. The authors acknowledge several limitations: focus on Python-based open-source contributions only; no analysis of code quality; potential measurement error biasing effects downward; no accounting for spillover effects between workers; and geographic limitations (China and Russia may have lower LLM use due to provider blocking and censorship). The study estimates that at 29% AI adoption rates, genAI would generate 23-38B USD of additional code per year (conservative baseline), with broader estimates of 38-167B USD when accounting for other studies' productivity effects. No arm-level sample sizes are specified as this is not a randomized controlled trial; instead, the study uses observational data from 100,097 US developers tracked over an average of 5.1 quarters.
